supported by comeback winner and match-winning goal in allowed_claims

goals value 1 means one goal; title does not claim a brace or two goals; body describes equaliser and winner, consistent with one goal scored plus assisted—check: goal value is 1, but action_notes say 'equalised then scored'; this would be two goals if both are his. The input shows goals: 1, but there is a match-winning goal (1) and a go-ahead goal (1); these are separate designations for the same goal? Actually allowed_claims include 'comeback win', 'go-ahead goal', 'match-winning goal'. However goals value is 1, so he scored one goal. But evidence says he equalised then scored winner; that would be two goals. Contradiction. Wait: action_notes say '66' winner' and 'scored the match-winning goal' but no second goal mention. 'equalised' might be via assist not goal. The key_metrics show goals=1, match_winning_goal=1, go_ahead_goal=1 (likely same goal). So he scored one goal that was both go-ahead and match-winning. He also had an assist. So 'equalised' is from assist. Correct. Body: scored the equaliser implies his goal? Actually 'scoring the equaliser then adding stoppage-time winner' implies he scored both; that would mean 2 goals. Risk: contradiction with goals=1. Revise body to avoid claiming two goals: 'scored the stoppage-time winner after helping Egypt equalise earlier' or similar. Adjust.
correct sequence: Egypt behind, then equalised (likely via his assist), then he scored winner. Yes.
